content.text: A single Cowork task runs sequentially from start to finish. When a problem is too large, too parallel, or too long for one sequential run, you decompose it into a network of coordinated tasks: an orchestrator that plans, subagent tasks that execute independently, and an aggregator that merges results. The workspace is the coordination bus between them.
What Subagents Are in Cowork

attrs.alt: Cowork subagent architecture: orchestrator fans out to parallel subagent tasks, aggregator collects results via shared workspace

content.text: Cowork does not have a native subagent API. A subagent is simply a regular Cowork task whose goal is written by another task (the orchestrator). The orchestrator writes task goal files to /workspace/tasks/, you trigger each subagent task manually or via scheduling, and results land in /workspace/out/. The aggregator task then reads all outputs and synthesizes the final deliverable.

This pattern works because the project workspace is shared and persistent. All tasks in the same project see the same /workspace/ directory, so writing a file in one task and reading it in another is the coordination mechanism — no API calls, no shared memory, no message queues.
When to Use Subagents
Subagents are appropriate when the work is genuinely parallel and each unit is independent. The decision criterion is whether the tasks can run in any order without affecting each other's output.
Situation
Use Subagents?
Rationale
Analyze 20 documents independently
Yes
Each document analysis is self-contained; order does not matter
Multi-step analysis where step B needs step A's output
No
Sequential dependency — use one task with multiple steps
Generate a section of a report for each region
Yes
Sections are independent; can be written in parallel and concatenated
Summarize a Slack thread
No
Single-source, single-output — one task is sufficient
Run the same workflow over a list of clients
Yes
Each client is isolated; parallelism reduces total wall time
Debug a multi-file codebase interactively
No
Requires iterative context across files — sequential reasoning
The Orchestrator Pattern

The orchestrator task is responsible for decomposing the problem and writing subagent goals. It reads the project context, determines the work units, and writes one goal file per subagent to /workspace/tasks/.
Orchestrator Goal Structure
The orchestrator goal describes what to decompose and where to write the subagent goals. It does not do any of the actual work.

Read /workspace/clients.csv (columns: client_id, client_name, report_period).
For each row, write a task goal file to /workspace/tasks/report-{client_id}.md.

Each goal file should contain:
- The client name and report period
- Instruction to read /workspace/data/{client_id}.csv
- Instruction to output a markdown report to /workspace/out/report-{client_id}.md
- Format rules: executive summary (3 bullets), then detailed metrics table

After writing all goal files, write /workspace/tasks/READY to signal completion.
Do not perform any analysis yourself.
Subagent Goal Files

Each goal file written by the orchestrator becomes the goal of one subagent task. The goal file should be self-contained — the subagent that reads it has the full project memory but should not need to read other files to understand what to do.

# Example: /workspace/tasks/report-acme-corp.md

Client: Acme Corp
Report period: Q2 2024

Read /workspace/data/acme-corp.csv and generate a revenue report.

Output to /workspace/out/report-acme-corp.md with:
- Executive summary: 3 bullet points on top findings
- Metrics table: revenue by month, MoM change, YoY change
- Risk flags: any month with >10% MoM decline

Do not summarize other clients. Focus only on Acme Corp data.
Write goal files so that a subagent with no knowledge of other subagents can execute them correctly. Avoid cross-references like 'also see task-b.md' — each goal must be fully self-contained.
Running Subagent Tasks
After the orchestrator writes all goal files, you create and run one Cowork task per goal file. Each subagent task uses the goal file as its task goal (paste or reference the file content).
Manual Triggering
For a small number of subagents (2–5), trigger each task manually in the Cowork interface. Use a consistent naming pattern so outputs are easy to identify and aggregate later.

# Naming pattern for subagent tasks:
report-acme-corp     -> goal: content of /workspace/tasks/report-acme-corp.md
report-globex        -> goal: content of /workspace/tasks/report-globex.md
report-initech       -> goal: content of /workspace/tasks/report-initech.md

# Each runs independently; start all before any finishes for true parallelism
Scheduled Triggering

For larger subagent networks (10+), use project scheduled tasks to trigger subagents on a staggered schedule. The orchestrator writes all goal files in one run; scheduled tasks fire at intervals to pick up and execute each one.

# Scheduled subagent pattern (project scheduled task goal):
Check /workspace/tasks/ for any .md file that does not yet have a corresponding
file in /workspace/out/.

If found:
  Read the first unclaimed goal file.
  Write /workspace/locks/{goal_filename}.lock to claim it (prevents duplicate runs).
  Execute the goal.
  Write output to /workspace/out/{client_id}.md.
  Delete the lock file.

If no unclaimed goal files exist, write nothing and exit.

# Run this scheduled task every 30 minutes until all outputs are written.
Isolation Between Subagents

Subagents do not communicate with each other directly. They should write to separate output paths and avoid reading each other's intermediate files. Isolation is enforced by convention: each subagent owns its output path (/workspace/out/result-{id}.md) and does not touch other paths.
Rule
Why It Matters
Each subagent writes to a unique output path
Prevents overwriting between concurrent tasks
Subagents do not read each other's outputs
Avoids cascading failures if one subagent errors
Subagent goals are self-contained
Any subagent can re-run without needing to know about others
Use a lock file if multiple subagents may claim the same work unit
Prevents duplicate processing of the same input
The Aggregator Pattern
Once all subagent tasks complete, the aggregator task reads all individual outputs and synthesizes the final deliverable. The aggregator is also a regular Cowork task.
Aggregator Goal Structure

All subagent reports are in /workspace/out/report-*.md.

Read each report and produce a consolidated executive summary:
- Combined metrics table: one row per client (client, Q2 revenue, MoM change, YoY change)
- Cross-client findings: top 3 clients by growth, bottom 3 by growth
- Risk flags: any client with two or more months of negative MoM growth

Output the consolidated report to /workspace/outputs/Q2_consolidated_report.md.

Do not re-analyze raw data. Use only the content in /workspace/out/report-*.md.
Detecting Completion Before Aggregating
Run the aggregator only after all subagents have written their outputs. A simple pre-condition check in the aggregator goal prevents premature aggregation.

# Aggregator with pre-condition check:
First, list all files in /workspace/tasks/ (expected goal files) and
all files in /workspace/out/ (completed outputs).

If the count of .md files in /workspace/out/ is less than the count in
/workspace/tasks/, stop and write:
'Not all subagents have completed. /workspace/out/ has N files; /workspace/tasks/ has M.
Re-run this aggregator task when all subagents are done.'

Only proceed with aggregation if counts match.
Workspace Layout for Subagent Workflows
A consistent workspace layout makes orchestrator and aggregator goals easier to write and reduces errors from mismatched paths.

/workspace/
  inputs/
    clients.csv          <- source data for orchestrator
    data/
      acme-corp.csv      <- per-client data, read by each subagent
      globex.csv
  tasks/
    report-acme-corp.md  <- goal file written by orchestrator
    report-globex.md
    READY                <- signal file: orchestrator done writing goals
  locks/
    report-acme-corp.md.lock  <- claimed by a subagent (prevents duplicates)
  out/
    report-acme-corp.md  <- output written by subagent A
    report-globex.md     <- output written by subagent B
  outputs/
    Q2_consolidated_report.md  <- final output from aggregator
Keep inputs/, tasks/, and out/ separate. This makes it easy to reset a workflow (delete tasks/ and out/) without losing source data.
Error Handling and Partial Failures
Subagent tasks can fail independently. A robust subagent workflow handles partial failures without requiring a full restart.
Detecting Failed Subagents
The aggregator can detect failures by comparing expected outputs to actual outputs. A missing output file signals that the corresponding subagent did not complete.

# Aggregator partial-failure handling:
For each goal file in /workspace/tasks/:
  Check if /workspace/out/{id}.md exists.

If any are missing, list them explicitly:
'The following subagents did not produce output:
  - report-acme-corp (missing /workspace/out/report-acme-corp.md)
  - report-initech (missing /workspace/out/report-initech.md)

Proceed with aggregation using only the N completed outputs.
Note the missing clients in the consolidated report under "Incomplete Data" section.'
Re-running Individual Subagents

Because each subagent is an independent task with its own goal file, re-running a failed subagent is straightforward: create a new Cowork task with the same goal content (from /workspace/tasks/{id}.md) and let it write to /workspace/out/{id}.md. No other subagents need to change.
Failure Type
Recovery Action
Subagent task failed mid-run
Re-run that subagent task only; other outputs are unaffected
Orchestrator wrote incomplete goal files
Fix the orchestrator goal, re-run it; delete existing task files first
Aggregator ran before all subagents finished
Re-run aggregator after remaining subagents complete
Lock file left behind (subagent crashed mid-lock)
Manually delete the stale .lock file, then re-run the subagent
Subagents vs Multi-Step Tasks
Not every complex task needs subagents. Subagents add coordination overhead; use them only when the parallelism benefit outweighs that cost.
Factor
Use Subagents
Use One Multi-Step Task
Work units
Independent, parallelizable
Sequential, each step depends on the prior
Volume
10+ units that would time out in one task
1–5 steps within a single task run
Error tolerance
Partial failure is acceptable
All-or-nothing; failure rolls back the whole task
Coordination complexity
Simple: workspace files as bus
High: subagents would need to read each other
Iteration
Each unit is well-defined upfront
Iterative; approach changes based on intermediate findings
Practical Limits and Considerations
Subagent workflows have operational constraints that affect design decisions.
Each subagent task consumes its own context window — project memory and workspace files are re-read for every subagent, which multiplies context cost.
Cowork does not run subagent tasks automatically; you trigger each one manually or via a scheduled task. Plan for the overhead of task creation.
Workspace file contention: if multiple subagents write to overlapping paths concurrently, outputs can be overwritten. Use unique output paths per subagent.
Large subagent networks (50+) become harder to monitor. Cap network size at what you can verify in the aggregator step.
Subagent tasks inherit the project's connector permissions. If a subagent needs a connector that is not set up, it will fail silently unless the goal includes a fallback instruction.

For workflows that run weekly or monthly, the orchestrator-subagent-aggregator pattern pairs naturally with scheduled tasks: orchestrator runs on day 1, subagents run over days 1-2, aggregator runs on day 3.
Subagent Workflow for Document Processing

Document processing — summarizing, extracting data, classifying, or translating a batch of files — is the most common subagent use case. The pattern is identical regardless of document type (PDF, CSV, markdown, email export).
Orchestrator: Write Per-Document Goals

Read /workspace/inputs/documents/ and list all .md files.
For each file, write /workspace/tasks/summarize-{filename}.md with the goal:

---
Summarize /workspace/inputs/documents/{filename}.
Extract: title, key decisions (bullet list), action items (bullet list with owner if mentioned).
Output to /workspace/out/summary-{filename}.md
Length: executive summary max 200 words; action items max 10 bullets.
---

Write /workspace/tasks/READY when all goal files are written.
Subagent: Execute the Per-Document Goal

# Content of /workspace/tasks/summarize-board-meeting-2024-06.md
Summarize /workspace/inputs/documents/board-meeting-2024-06.md.
Extract: title, key decisions (bullet list), action items (bullet list with owner if mentioned).
Output to /workspace/out/summary-board-meeting-2024-06.md
Length: executive summary max 200 words; action items max 10 bullets.
Aggregator: Merge All Summaries

Read all files in /workspace/out/summary-*.md.
Produce a combined digest:
- Section 'Key Decisions This Month': deduplicated bullet list from all summaries
- Section 'Open Action Items': table with columns (item, owner, source document)
- Section 'Document Index': one row per summarized document with title and word count

Output to /workspace/outputs/monthly-digest.md
Keep the aggregator goal focused on merging, not re-analyzing. The subagents did the analysis; the aggregator's job is synthesis and deduplication only.
Monitoring Subagent Progress

Because subagents run as separate tasks, progress monitoring is manual. Cowork does not provide a built-in workflow view across tasks in the same project. These patterns help track progress without constant manual checking.
Status File Pattern
Have each subagent write a one-line status file in addition to its output. The status file lets you verify completion without opening each output file.

# Append to each subagent goal:
After writing the output file, write /workspace/status/{id}.done
with content: 'DONE: {id} completed at step N'

# Orchestrator or aggregator checks progress:
Count files in /workspace/status/ versus files in /workspace/tasks/.
Report: 'N of M subagents completed' before proceeding.
Checkpoint Aggregation

For long-running workflows where subagents complete over hours or days, run the aggregator in checkpoint mode: aggregate whatever is available now and note what is still pending. This produces a partial deliverable that is useful even before all subagents finish.

# Aggregator checkpoint goal:
List all files in /workspace/out/. Aggregate whatever exists.
In the output header, note: 'Partial report as of [today]. N of M clients included.
Missing: [list client IDs with no output file]. Re-run aggregator when all are complete.'
Monitoring Pattern
When to Use
Downside
Status files (/workspace/status/*.done)
Any workflow; easiest to implement
Adds a small write step to every subagent goal
Checkpoint aggregation
Long workflows where partial results are useful
Aggregator must be re-run when remaining subagents finish
Count outputs vs tasks
Simple check; no extra writes needed
Cannot distinguish failed from not-yet-started
Lock files (/workspace/locks/*.lock)
Shared queue; prevent duplicate claims
Stale locks if subagent crashes; need manual cleanup
Combining Subagents with Connectors
Subagents can use connectors just like any other task. This enables workflows where each subagent fetches data from an external source, processes it, and writes a result — all independently.

# Subagent goal that uses a connector:
Fetch Jira issues assigned to team member {name} from the current sprint
(connector: Jira, project: ANALYTICS, sprint: active).

For each issue:
- Status, summary, and story points
- Number of comments
- Days since last update

Output a markdown table to /workspace/out/jira-report-{name}.md
If the connector returns no issues, write 'No active issues for {name}' to the output file.

When subagents use connectors, each subagent makes its own connector calls independently. There is no shared connector session across subagents. Scope each subagent's connector query narrowly to avoid fetching overlapping data that bloats multiple context windows.
Summary
Cowork subagents are regular tasks whose goals are written by an orchestrator task and whose outputs are read by an aggregator task.
The workspace (/workspace/) is the coordination bus — subagents do not communicate directly with each other or with the orchestrator at runtime.
Use subagents when work units are genuinely independent and parallel; use multi-step single tasks when steps depend on each other.
Each subagent goal file must be fully self-contained — no cross-references to other subagent files.
The aggregator should verify all subagent outputs exist before aggregating and handle partial failures explicitly.
Use unique output paths per subagent and lock files when subagents may claim the same work unit from a shared queue.
Subagents work best for well-defined, repeatable workflows; avoid them for exploratory or highly iterative tasks.
